questions.prompt: A disease affects 1 in 100 people. A test is 99% sensitive (flags 99% of the sick) and 99% specific (clears 99% of the healthy). You test positive. What is the probability you actually have the condition, given the positive result? Give the exact fraction, and explain whether it should worry you.
questions.source: The canonical base-rate / PPV interview question. Sources: quantblueprint.com/glossary/bayes-theorem; nishchalnishant.gitbook.io 'Canonical Stats Questions' (both state 50%). GB-anchored: Green Book p.37–38 §Conditional Probability & Bayes' Formula.

approaches: Natural frequencies: imagine 10,000 people; split into true positives = prevalence·sensitivity·N and false positives = (1−prevalence)·(1−specificity)·N; PPV = TP/(TP+FP).
Bayes formula: P(sick|+) = p·sens / (p·sens + (1−p)·(1−spec)).
Odds form: prior odds p/(1−p) × likelihood ratio sens/(1−spec), then convert odds→probability.
wrongTurns: Answering with the test accuracy itself ("99% accurate ⇒ 99% sick") — confusing P(+|sick) with P(sick|+).
Ignoring the base rate / treating false positives as negligible.
Forgetting to renormalize by the total probability of a positive result.
hintLadder: What is the population actually being tested, and how many of THEM are sick before any test runs?
Out of (say) 10,000 people, count the true positives and the false positives separately — which group is bigger?
PPV = true positives ÷ (true positives + false positives). Plug in prevalence·sens and (1−prevalence)·(1−spec).

rubric.correctness: Lands on the exact posterior 1/2 as a reduced fraction (not a decimal-only guess, not an off-by-normalization value).
rubric.approach: Separates prior from likelihood and applies Bayes correctly (PPV = TP/(TP+FP), i.e. p·sens / (p·sens + (1−p)(1−spec))); never silently swaps P(evidence|hypothesis) for P(hypothesis|evidence).
rubric.rigor: Carries exact rationals end-to-end, normalizes by the total probability of the evidence, and sanity-checks the result against the base rate (does it move in the right direction, by a believable amount?).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, labels prior / likelihood / posterior, and can say in words WHY the number is surprising or not.
rubric.speed: Reaches 1/2 without thrashing; a strong candidate reaches for the natural-frequency or odds-form shortcut quickly.

followUps: How low must the false-positive rate go before a positive means >90%?
Re-derive it with a confusion grid over 10,000 people — do the counts agree?
If a second independent positive arrives, where does the probability go?

questions.prompt: A spam filter catches 99% of spam (recall) but also flags 2% of legitimate mail (false-positive rate). 20% of incoming mail is spam. An email lands in the spam folder. What is the probability the flagged item is the positive class (the model's precision on this flag)? Give the exact fraction and say whether you'd ship this alert to a human.
questions.source: Precision under class imbalance (false-positive paradox), spam-filter example: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Base_rate_fallacy. GB-anchored: Green Book p.37 §Bayes' Formula.

approaches: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = baseRate·recall / (baseRate·recall + (1−baseRate)·FPR) — the PPV formula in ML clothing.
Natural frequencies over 10,000 items: count flagged-and-positive vs flagged-and-negative.
Odds form: prior odds × (recall / FPR), then odds→probability.
wrongTurns: Reporting recall or accuracy as if it were precision.
Assuming a low FPR is "good enough" without weighing it against the rare positive class.
Averaging recall and specificity instead of normalizing.
hintLadder: Precision asks: of everything we FLAGGED, what fraction is truly positive? Start from how many of each class exist.
Per 10,000 items, compute true flags (baseRate·recall·N) and false flags ((1−baseRate)·FPR·N).
Precision = trueFlags / (trueFlags + falseFlags). Substitute the base rate, recall, and FPR.

rubric.correctness: Lands on the exact posterior 99/107 as a reduced fraction (not a decimal-only guess, not an off-by-normalization value).
rubric.approach: Separates prior from likelihood and applies Bayes correctly (precision = TP/(TP+FP) under the given base rate); never silently swaps P(evidence|hypothesis) for P(hypothesis|evidence).
rubric.rigor: Carries exact rationals end-to-end, normalizes by the total probability of the evidence, and sanity-checks the result against the base rate (does it move in the right direction, by a believable amount?).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, labels prior / likelihood / posterior, and can say in words WHY the number is surprising or not.
rubric.speed: Reaches 99/107 without thrashing; a strong candidate reaches for the natural-frequency or odds-form shortcut quickly.

followUps: How does precision move if you double the base rate (e.g. by pre-filtering the stream)?
Trade recall for specificity — what happens to precision, and what is the operating-point intuition?
If two independent models both flag the item, what is the combined precision?

questions.prompt: Two coins sit in a bag: one fair, one double-headed (heads on both sides). You draw one at random and flip it, getting heads. What is the probability you are holding the double-headed coin? Exact fraction.
questions.source: Two-coin Bayes classic: stats.stackexchange.com/questions/514627; mathproblems.info/prob16s.htm (both state 2/3 for one head). GB-anchored: Green Book p.38 §"Unfair coin".

approaches: Likelihoods: double-headed predicts k heads with probability 1; fair predicts them with probability (1/2)^k.
Odds form: prior odds 1:1, each head multiplies the odds by the likelihood ratio 2; after k heads odds are 2^k:1.
wrongTurns: Saying "a head can come from either coin, so it is still 1/2" — ignores that the two coins predict heads at different rates.

hintLadder: How often does EACH coin produce this exact run of heads?
Weight each coin (1/2 prior) by how well it predicted the heads, then renormalize.
Posterior = 2^k / (2^k + 1); substitute k.

questions.prompt: You are given 1000 coins. Exactly one is double-headed; the other 999 are fair. You pick one at random and toss it 1 time — every toss is heads. What is the probability you chose the double-headed coin? Exact fraction.
questions.source: Green Book p.38 §"Unfair coin" (1000 coins, one double-headed, 10 heads → 1024/2023), generalized to N coins and k heads.

approaches: Prior odds (double-headed : fair) = 1 : (N−1). Each head multiplies the odds by 2 (likelihood ratio 1 ÷ 1/2).
After k heads: odds = 2^k : (N−1), so P = 2^k / (2^k + (N−1)).
Equivalently bayesUpdate with prior 1/N, likelihoods 1 and (1/2)^k, then renormalize.
wrongTurns: Concluding the coin "must" be double-headed after a long head run, forgetting the (N−1)-to-1 prior against it.
Using k heads as the likelihood ratio instead of 2^k.
Treating the rare prior as if it were even.
hintLadder: Before any toss, how much more likely is a fair coin than the double-headed one?
Each head is exactly twice as likely under the double-headed coin — how do k heads compound that?
Posterior odds = 2^k : (N−1); convert to a probability with N=1000, k=1.

questions.prompt: A family has two children. You learn that at least one of them is a boy. What is the probability that both children are boys? Exact fraction, and explain the subtlety.

wrongTurns: Assuming "at least one boy" and "a specific child is a boy" give the same answer.

Forgetting to renormalize over the surviving outcomes.

Cross out the orders the evidence rules out — how many remain, and how many are all-boys?
Probability = (all-boys orders) ÷ (orders consistent with the evidence).

The base rate (only 15% Blue) drags the answer well below the witness's 80% reliability.

questions.prompt: You have 1,000 coins: one is double-headed, the other 999 are fair. You pick one at random and start flipping; it keeps coming up heads. What is the SMALLEST number of consecutive heads after which the chosen coin is more likely than not to be the double-headed one? Justify the threshold.
questions.source: Green Book p.38 §"Unfair coin" (solve 2^k > 999 ⇒ k = 10; the 10th head is the one that crosses ½).

approaches: Posterior after k heads = 2^k/(2^k+999); it exceeds 1/2 exactly when 2^k > 999.
2^9 = 512 < 999 < 1024 = 2^10, so the 10th head is the first to tip past even odds.
Odds view: each head doubles the odds from the 1:999 start; you need 10 doublings to pass 1:1.
wrongTurns: Guessing a much larger k (assuming a 1-in-1000 prior needs hundreds of heads).
Solving 2^k > 1000 and getting 10 for the wrong reason, or 2^k ≥ 999 sloppiness.
Treating each head as additive rather than doubling the odds.
hintLadder: Write the posterior after k heads as a function of k.
It beats 1/2 exactly when the numerator passes the leftover 999 — i.e. 2^k > 999.
Find the smallest power of two above 999.

rubric.correctness: Lands on the exact posterior 10 as a reduced fraction (not a decimal-only guess, not an off-by-normalization value).
rubric.approach: Separates prior from likelihood and applies Bayes correctly (argmin over k of sequentialPosterior crossing 1/2, i.e. 2^k > 999); never silently swaps P(evidence|hypothesis) for P(hypothesis|evidence).
rubric.rigor: Carries exact rationals end-to-end, normalizes by the total probability of the evidence, and sanity-checks the result against the base rate (does it move in the right direction, by a believable amount?).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, labels prior / likelihood / posterior, and can say in words WHY the number is surprising or not.
rubric.speed: Reaches 10 without thrashing; a strong candidate reaches for the natural-frequency or odds-form shortcut quickly.

followUps: How many heads to be 99% sure instead of just more-likely-than-not?
Redo it for 1,000,000 coins — how does the threshold scale with the number of coins?
Why does the required k grow only logarithmically in the number of coins?

questions.prompt: Same setup — 1,000 coins, one double-headed, 999 fair, chosen at random and flipping heads repeatedly. What is the smallest number of consecutive heads after which you are at least 99% sure you hold the double-headed coin?
questions.source: Green Book p.38 §"Unfair coin", extended (solve 2^k/(2^k+999) ≥ 99/100 ⇒ 2^k ≥ 98,901 ⇒ k = 17).

approaches: Odds view: need final odds ≥ 99:1, i.e. 2^k/999 ≥ 99, i.e. 2^k ≥ 98,901.
wrongTurns: Stopping at the k that just crosses 1/2 (k = 10) and calling it "sure".
Setting 2^k ≥ 99·999 but mishandling the algebra.
Linear extrapolation from the 50% threshold.
hintLadder: Turn "99% sure" into a condition on the posterior odds (final odds ≥ 99:1).
Find the smallest power of two at least 98,901.

rubric.correctness: Lands on the exact posterior 17 as a reduced fraction (not a decimal-only guess, not an off-by-normalization value).
rubric.approach: Separates prior from likelihood and applies Bayes correctly (argmin over k of sequentialPosterior crossing 99/100, i.e. 2^k ≥ 98,901); never silently swaps P(evidence|hypothesis) for P(hypothesis|evidence).
rubric.rigor: Carries exact rationals end-to-end, normalizes by the total probability of the evidence, and sanity-checks the result against the base rate (does it move in the right direction, by a believable amount?).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, labels prior / likelihood / posterior, and can say in words WHY the number is surprising or not.
rubric.speed: Reaches 17 without thrashing; a strong candidate reaches for the natural-frequency or odds-form shortcut quickly.

followUps: Compare: 10 heads to pass 50%, 17 to pass 99% — why so few extra for so much more certainty?
What threshold of certainty would require 20 heads?
Generalize the "k to reach confidence c" formula in terms of the number of coins.

questions.prompt: A DNA test has a random-match probability of 1 in 1,000,000 and never misses a true match. A suspect is one of 10,001 people who could equally have been the source (so your prior that this particular person is the source is 1/10,001). The suspect's DNA matches. What is the probability the suspect is actually the source? Exact fraction — and name the fallacy if you answered "1 − 1/1,000,000".
questions.source: Prosecutor's fallacy / cold-hit DNA Bayes problem. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prosecutor%27s_fallacy. GB-anchored: Green Book p.37 §Bayes' Formula.

approaches: Bayes with prior 1/10,001: P(source|match) = p·1 / (p·1 + (1−p)·RMP).
Natural frequencies: 1 true source matches for sure; ~10,000 innocents each match with probability 1/1,000,000 ⇒ ~0.01 expected false match.
Odds view: prior odds 1:10,000, likelihood ratio 1 ÷ (1/1,000,000) = 1,000,000 ⇒ posterior odds 100:1.
wrongTurns: Reporting ≈ 1 − 1/1,000,000 by reading the random-match probability as P(innocent|match) — the prosecutor's fallacy.
Dropping the prior / pool size entirely.
Confusing P(match|innocent) with P(innocent|match).
hintLadder: The 1-in-a-million is P(match | innocent), not P(innocent | match).
Out of ~10,000 innocents, how many would match by chance? Compare that to the one guaranteed true match.
Posterior odds = prior odds 1:10,000 × likelihood ratio 1,000,000 = 100:1.

rubric.correctness: Lands on the exact posterior 100/101 as a reduced fraction (not a decimal-only guess, not an off-by-normalization value).
rubric.approach: Separates prior from likelihood and applies Bayes correctly (bayesUpdate with the pool-size prior and the random-match probability as P(match|innocent)); never silently swaps P(evidence|hypothesis) for P(hypothesis|evidence).
rubric.rigor: Carries exact rationals end-to-end, normalizes by the total probability of the evidence, and sanity-checks the result against the base rate (does it move in the right direction, by a believable amount?).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, labels prior / likelihood / posterior, and can say in words WHY the number is surprising or not.
rubric.speed: Reaches 100/101 without thrashing; a strong candidate reaches for the natural-frequency or odds-form shortcut quickly.

followUps: How does the answer change if the innocent pool is 1,000,000 instead of 10,000?
Why does database size matter so much in cold-hit cases?
Articulate precisely why "1 − RMP" is the wrong conditional.

questions.prompt: Three doors; a car behind one, goats behind the other two. You pick Door 1. The host — who knows where the car is and always opens a different door revealing a goat — opens Door 3. Treating the host's choice as evidence, what is the probability the car is behind Door 2 (i.e. that switching wins)? Exact fraction, with the conditioning made explicit.
questions.source: Monty Hall problem (host's action is informative). Green Book p.40 (Monty Hall → 2/3); en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monty_Hall_problem.

approaches: Likelihood of the host opening Door 3: 1/2 if car is behind Door 1, 1 if behind Door 2, 0 if behind Door 3.
bayesPosterior([1/3,1/3,1/3], [1/2,1,0]) gives P(Door2) = 2/3.
Intuition: your Door 1 had prior 1/3; the host's constrained reveal pours the rest onto Door 2.
wrongTurns: Claiming 1/2 because "two doors remain" — ignores that the host's choice is constrained by the car's location.
Using a uniform likelihood for the host's action.
Forgetting the host can never open the door hiding the car.
hintLadder: The host's choice is not random — it depends on where the car is. Write P(opens Door 3 | car at each door).
Weight each door's 1/3 prior by that likelihood and renormalize.
The car-at-Door-3 branch has likelihood 0; compare the Door-1 (1/2) and Door-2 (1) branches.

rubric.correctness: Lands on the exact posterior 2/3 as a reduced fraction (not a decimal-only guess, not an off-by-normalization value).
rubric.approach: Separates prior from likelihood and applies Bayes correctly (bayesPosterior over door locations with the host's constrained reveal as the likelihood); never silently swaps P(evidence|hypothesis) for P(hypothesis|evidence).
rubric.rigor: Carries exact rationals end-to-end, normalizes by the total probability of the evidence, and sanity-checks the result against the base rate (does it move in the right direction, by a believable amount?).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, labels prior / likelihood / posterior, and can say in words WHY the number is surprising or not.
rubric.speed: Reaches 2/3 without thrashing; a strong candidate reaches for the natural-frequency or odds-form shortcut quickly.

followUps: What if the host sometimes opens YOUR door, or opens at random — does switching still help?
Generalize to 100 doors with the host opening 98 goats.
Why does the answer hinge on the host's knowledge rather than the count of remaining doors?

questions.prompt: Three cards: one black on both sides (BB), one white on both sides (WW), one black on one side and white on the other (BW). You draw a card at random, look at one uniformly-random face, and it is black. What is the probability the OTHER side is also black? Exact fraction — beware the "two cards left, so 1/2" trap.
questions.source: Bertrand's box / three-card paradox. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bertrand%27s_box_paradox. GB-anchored: Green Book p.37 §Bayes' Formula.

approaches: Likelihood of "shown face is black": 1 for BB, 1/2 for BW, 0 for WW.
bayesPosterior([1/3,1/3,1/3], [1,1/2,0]) gives P(BB) = 2/3; the other side is black iff the card is BB.
Count black FACES, not cards: 3 black faces are possible, 2 of them sit on the BB card.
wrongTurns: Saying 1/2 ("it's either BB or BW") — ignores that BB offers two ways to show a black face.
Conditioning on the card rather than on the observed face.
Giving the WW card nonzero weight after seeing black.
hintLadder: Think in faces, not cards: how many black faces are there, and which card are they on?
BB can show black two ways; BW only one — weight accordingly.
P(BB | black face) = (1) ÷ (1 + 1/2 + 0) over the priors = 2/3.

rubric.correctness: Lands on the exact posterior 2/3 as a reduced fraction (not a decimal-only guess, not an off-by-normalization value).
rubric.approach: Separates prior from likelihood and applies Bayes correctly (bayesPosterior over card identity with the shown-face color as the likelihood); never silently swaps P(evidence|hypothesis) for P(hypothesis|evidence).
rubric.rigor: Carries exact rationals end-to-end, normalizes by the total probability of the evidence, and sanity-checks the result against the base rate (does it move in the right direction, by a believable amount?).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, labels prior / likelihood / posterior, and can say in words WHY the number is surprising or not.
rubric.speed: Reaches 2/3 without thrashing; a strong candidate reaches for the natural-frequency or odds-form shortcut quickly.

followUps: Reframe entirely in terms of equally-likely faces — why is that the cleanest derivation?
How is this structurally identical to the two-children "specific boy" problem?
Generalize to n cards with mixed face colors.

interviewerPrompt:
# Bayes' Rule — AI Quant Interviewer (interviewer prompt template)

You are a SENIOR QUANTITATIVE INTERVIEWER at a top trading desk (think Jane Street / Citadel / IMC), running a live probability interview on conditional probability and Bayes' rule. You are professional, probing, and fair — but you keep the candidate under realistic time pressure and you do not hand out the answer.

The platform injects ONE question per turn from the engine-verified pool (or the generator). For the current question you receive, as ground truth:
- PROMPT: {{PROMPT}}
- TIER: {{TIER}}            (hard | harder | brutal)
- SOURCE: {{SOURCE}}        (context only — do not read it aloud)
- HIDDEN.ANSWER: {{HIDDEN_ANSWER}}            (exact fraction; NEVER reveal)
- HIDDEN.APPROACHES: {{HIDDEN_APPROACHES}}    (accepted solution paths)
- HIDDEN.WRONG_TURNS: {{HIDDEN_WRONG_TURNS}}  (common misconceptions to watch for)
- HIDDEN.HINT_LADDER: {{HIDDEN_HINT_LADDER}}  (rung 1 nudge → rung 2 stronger → rung 3 near-reveal)
- HIDDEN.RUBRIC: {{HIDDEN_RUBRIC}}            (correctness / approach / rigor / communication / speed)
- FOLLOW_UPS: {{FOLLOW_UPS}}

## Grounding clause (CRITICAL — keeps you honest)
Treat HIDDEN.ANSWER and HIDDEN.APPROACHES as the ONLY source of mathematical truth. Do NOT re-derive, re-compute, or "double-check" the math yourself — your arithmetic is not trusted; the engine-verified record is. Grade the candidate strictly against HIDDEN.RUBRIC. If the candidate's exact fraction equals HIDDEN.ANSWER (in any equivalent reduced form), it is correct; if it does not, it is not — never argue them into or out of the engine's answer with your own derivation.

## Protocol
1. Present exactly ONE question at a time — read PROMPT verbatim, then stop and let them work.
2. Make them THINK ALOUD. Ask "what's your prior?", "what's the likelihood of this evidence under each hypothesis?", "what are you conditioning on?" Insist on reasoning, not just a number.
3. NEVER reveal the answer, the fraction, or any rung of the hint ladder unprompted. Do not confirm partial numbers early.
4. Probe assumptions and edge cases: independence, what the evidence actually says (P(E|H) vs P(H|E)), base rates, normalization, and whether the magnitude is believable.
5. Escalating hints ONLY when the candidate is genuinely stuck (silent, going in circles, or has asked). Release HINT_LADDER strictly in order — rung one first, then rung two, then rung three — each only after further struggle. Reaching the final near-reveal rung should cost them on the "speed"/"approach" rubric dimensions.
6. When they commit to an exact fraction, evaluate it against HIDDEN.ANSWER. If wrong, identify which WRONG_TURN they hit and ask a targeted question that exposes it — do not lecture.
7. On a correct, well-justified answer, ask the FOLLOW_UPS in order to push toward the next tier (generalize, add evidence, adversarial edge case). Keep escalating until time or the candidate's depth runs out.
8. CLOSE with structured feedback: one line per rubric dimension (correctness, approach, rigor, communication, speed), each rated Strong / Solid / Shaky / Missing with a one-clause reason, then a single overall score out of 10 and one concrete thing to improve. Base every rating ONLY on HIDDEN.RUBRIC.

## Style
Tight, desk-floor register. No filler, no flattery, no emoji. Reward candidates who reach for natural frequencies, the odds form (prior odds × likelihood ratio), or a clean normalization — that is the quant tell. Penalize base-rate neglect and P(E|H)/P(H|E) confusion.

## Injection
At runtime, replace every {{...}} placeholder with the drawn question's fields. This template itself contains NO answers — keep it that way.
generatorPrompt:
# Bayes' Rule — runtime question GENERATOR prompt

Generate ONE fresh, non-overlapping quant-interview question on conditional probability / Bayes' rule, ready to engine-verify and serve. This is the ONLY place the factory is allowed to invent a question — so it is fenced by two non-negotiable rules: real-quant-style AND engine-verify-before-serve.

## Hard constraints
1. REAL QUANT-STYLE ONLY. The question must read like something actually asked on a quant/ML interview, anchored to the Green Book conditional-probability/Bayes topic (Green Book Ch.4, p.37–42) and the quant-interview canon. NEVER invent an arbitrary engine-solvable puzzle that merely happens to verify. If you cannot anchor it to a recognized question family (below) or a sourced real interview question, do not emit it.
2. PREFER PARAMETERIZING AN ENGINE-BACKED TEMPLATE. Only fall back to a free-form question when a template genuinely cannot express it, and even then it must be a sourced, canonical Bayes question.
3. ENGINE-VERIFY-BEFORE-SERVE. Output the data needed to recompute the answer with src/engine/bayes.ts. The serving feature MUST run the engine on (engineCheck.fn, engineCheck.args), compare formatRational(result) to engineCheck.answer, and REJECT the question if they differ or if the engine call throws. Never show an unverified question to a candidate.
4. NO OVERLAP, EVER (per student). You are given AVOID_LIST = {{AVOID_LIST}} (the student's already-seen fingerprints + the global pool fingerprints). Produce a question whose fingerprint is NOT in AVOID_LIST — vary the scenario AND the numbers, not just the wording. A reworded or parameter-trivial duplicate is a failure.
5. EXACT RATIONALS ONLY. Every probability is an exact reduced fraction (use formatRational); choose parameters that stay well under 2^53 in any intermediate product. No decimals-as-truth, no floats.
6. DIFFICULTY FLOOR = hard. Tag tier ∈ {hard, harder, brutal}; never below the lessons' mastery challenges. Always attach a follow-up chain.

## Engine-backed templates (prefer these). engineCheck.fn ∈ {bayesUpdate, bayesPosterior, oddsUpdateProb, sequentialPosterior, naturalFrequencies, smallestKCross}:
- screening-ppv: bayesUpdate(prevalence, sensitivity, 1−specificity) — P(condition | positive test) — base-rate / PPV screening
- spam-precision: bayesUpdate(baseRate, recall, falsePositiveRate) — precision of a flag under class imbalance (spam / fraud / moderation / anomaly)
- two-coins-sequential: sequentialPosterior(1/2, 1, 1/2, k) — fair vs double-headed coin after k heads → 2^k/(2^k+1)
- rare-coin-n: sequentialPosterior(1/N, 1, 1/2, k) — N coins, one double-headed, k heads → 2^k/(2^k+N−1) (GB 1000-coins)
- odds-multi-evidence: oddsToProb(∏ posteriorOdds(priorOdds, LR_i)) — stacking independent tests/signals in odds form (LR+ and LR−)
- two-children: bayesUpdate(1/2^n, 1, P(≥1 boy | not all boys)) — Green Book boys-and-girls conditioning (≥1 boy vs a specific boy)
- two-urns: bayesUpdate(P(A), P(red|A), P(red|B)) — pick an urn, draw a color, posterior on the urn
- multi-source-defect: bayesPosterior(shares, defectRates)[j] — n factories/machines/suppliers, a defective item → P(source j)
- natural-frequency-counts: naturalFrequencies(prevalence, sensitivity, specificity, population)[cell] — confusion-grid counts and PPV as whole numbers
